narrative: I'm sorry to hear that your law school application was rejected—it's tough news, and I want to help you understand why in a straightforward way. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for your Year 1 school grades, the second-bottom decile for Year 3, an LSAT score of 28.3, an undergraduate GPA of 2.8, upper-middle family income, identifying as male and White, and planning to attend full-time. These details were fed into a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model estimated a 53.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam initially, which tipped the scales just over the threshold for rejection, as schools prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed professionally. Looking at what drove this prediction, your Year 3 class decile rank was the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, since it was much lower than typical students' ranks. Right behind that was your even lower Year 1 decile rank, which also strongly nudged the prediction in the unfavorable direction compared to averages. Your LSAT score came next in influence, pulling toward failure because it fell well below the dataset average of 36.7. Family income followed closely, with your upper-middle bracket having a modest push in the same direction relative to peers. Your undergraduate GPA exerted similar influence, as 2.8 is lower than the typical 3.21 seen in the data. Other aspects like race, full-time status, and gender had the least sway but still slightly contributed toward the failure prediction, aligning with common distributions in the dataset. Overall, your early academic deciles, LSAT, and GPA were the top-ranked concerns, all below-average and reinforcing each other. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim higher through targeted prep, build stronger study habits proven to lift bar passage rates, and seek experiences that demonstrate academic turnaround since your school deciles reflect past performance. With effort in these areas, you could shift future predictions in your favor—many students improve dramatically with focused preparation. Hang in there; this is just one data point, not your full story.
